steps.title: The Legal Hierarchy
steps.objective: Define the fundamental difference between statutory and non-statutory requirements.
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament; non-statutory regulations are codes of practice or guidance.
Electricians must know which documents are legally mandatory versus those that provide technical guidance to meet the law.

In the UK electrical industry, documents are split into two main groups based on their legal power. Statutory regulations are the 'law of the land.' These are Acts of Parliament that you must follow by law; they are mandatory. If you break these, you are committing a criminal offense which can lead to legal action in a court of law.

On the other hand, non-statutory regulations are 'codes of practice' or technical guidance. These are not laws themselves, but they provide the specific instructions on how to work safely to meet the requirements of the law. Think of statutory regulations as the 'what you must do' (for example, keep people safe) and non-statutory as the 'how to do it' (for example, what size cable to use for a specific circuit). While statutory is the legal framework, non-statutory is the industry's technical manual.

steps.title: Legal Status vs. Technical Guidance
steps.objective: Explain how non-statutory guidance supports statutory requirements.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory documents like BS 7671 are used to demonstrate compliance with statutory laws like the EAWR.
It prevents the mistake of thinking guidance is 'optional' just because it isn't a direct Act of Parliament.

Even though non-statutory documents like BS 7671 (the IET Wiring Regulations) are not Acts of Parliament, they are not 'optional' in a practical sense. They are used in court to show whether an electrician has met the legal standards set by statutory laws. For example, the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is a statutory law that says electrical systems must be constructed to prevent danger. However, the EAWR does not tell you exactly how many sockets to put on a circuit or how deep to bury a cable.

To meet that legal requirement, you follow the non-statutory guidance in BS 7671. If an accident occurs and a case goes to court, a judge will look at whether you followed the non-statutory guidance to decide if you obeyed the statutory law. Following the guidance is the primary way to prove you are working to a safe, legal standard.

steps.title: Identifying Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Identify specific statutory regulations relevant to the electrical industry.
keyTerms: Health and Safety at Work Act
keyIdeas: HASAWA, EAWR, ESQCR, PUWER, COSHH, CDM, Manual Handling, PPE, Noise at Work, Environmental Act, DDA, and Equal Opportunities.
Failure to comply with these specific Acts can lead to criminal prosecution.

You must recognize specific statutory regulations by their names. The most important is the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), which is the 'umbrella' law for all workplace safety. Underneath this, we have the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), which is the primary law for our trade.

Other statutory laws you must identify include: PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) for using tools safely; COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) for handling chemicals or dust; and the PPE (Personal Protective Equipment at Work) Regulations. You also need to know the ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations), which concerns the supply of electricity, and the CDM (Construction Design and Management) Regulations. Other examples include the Environmental Act, the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act), and the Manual Handling Operations Regulations. All of these are mandatory legal requirements.
Teach the core idea: statutory regulations.

steps.title: Identifying Non-Statutory Guidance
steps.objective: Identify specific non-statutory regulations and guidance notes.
keyIdeas: BS 7671 (IET Wiring Regulations), On-Site Guide, Unite Union Book, Guidance Notes, and Codes of Practice.
These are the primary technical tools used daily for design, installation, and testing.

Non-statutory documents are the technical tools you use every day to design and install systems. The most famous is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' Because it is a British Standard (BS), it is non-statutory, not a law. Another vital document is the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified version of BS 7671 for common domestic and small commercial jobs.

You will also use Guidance Notes (GN), such as Guidance Note 3, which focuses specifically on inspection and testing. Other non-statutory sources include the Unite Union Book (often used for site rates and conditions) and various industry Codes of Practice. These documents provide the 'how-to' details that the statutory laws leave out. If a document is published by the IET (Institution of Engineering and Technology) or the BSI (British Standards Institution), it is almost always non-statutory guidance.
Teach the core idea: non-statutory regulations/guidance.

steps.title: Categorization Mastery
steps.objective: Correctly sort a mixed list of industry documents into statutory and non-statutory categories.

Ensures the learner can accurately identify the nature of any document they encounter on site.

To tell the difference quickly, look at the titles and the purpose of the document. Statutory regulations usually end in 'Act' or 'Regulations' and are issued by the government to set a legal minimum. Examples include the Noise at Work Regulations and the Equal Opportunities legislation. These focus on the legal duties of employers and employees.

Non-statutory documents are often technical manuals. While the EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) is a statutory law, the 'Wiring Regulations' (BS 7671) is a non-statutory standard. This is a common point of confusion: just because BS 7671 has the word 'Regulations' in its title, it does not make it a statutory Act of Parliament. Similarly, any document labeled as a 'Guidance Note' or 'Code of Practice' is non-statutory. Keeping these categories clear ensures you know which document provides the mandatory legal framework and which provides the technical instruction needed to comply with that framework.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to classify the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and BS 7671 by looking at their legal origin and purpose.
keyIdeas: Demonstrate how to classify the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and BS 7671 by looking at their legal origin and purpose.
Show one worked example.
Scenario: You are preparing for a site inspection and need to explain to an apprentice why we use both the 'Electricity at Work Regulations' (EAWR) and 'BS 7671' (The Wiring Regulations).

Step 1: Identify the legal origin. Look at who issued the document. The EAWR is a set of regulations passed by Parliament, making it an Act of the land. BS 7671 is published by the Institution of Engineering and Technology (IET) and the British Standards Institution (BSI).

Step 2: Determine the purpose. Ask yourself: 'Does this set a broad legal duty or a specific technical instruction?' The EAWR states that systems must be safe to prevent danger (a broad legal duty). BS 7671 gives the specific numbers, such as disconnection times and cable capacities (technical instructions).

Step 3: Categorize the documents. Because the EAWR is a mandatory law from Parliament, it is Statutory. Because BS 7671 is a technical standard published by industry bodies to provide guidance, it is Non-statutory.

terms.simpleDefinition: Laws passed by Parliament that are mandatory and must be followed to avoid criminal prosecution.
terms.misconception: Thinking these are just 'best practice' suggestions rather than legal requirements.

terms.simpleDefinition: Technical guides and codes of practice that provide instructions on how to meet the requirements of the law.
terms.misconception: Believing that because they aren't laws, they can be ignored without any legal consequence in court.

terms.term: HASAWA (Health and Safety at Work Act)
terms.simpleDefinition: The primary UK law that sets out the general duties of employers and employees for safety at work.
terms.anchor: The umbrella law for all work safety.
terms.misconception: Thinking it only applies to dangerous construction sites rather than every workplace.

terms.simpleDefinition: The specific statutory law that mandates safety requirements for all electrical systems and work activities.
terms.misconception: Confusing these legal requirements with the technical rules found in BS 7671.

terms.simpleDefinition: The main non-statutory national standard for the design, installation, and testing of electrical wiring.
terms.misconception: Believing this is an actual Act of Parliament; it is a standard, not a law.

terms.simpleDefinition: A non-statutory handbook that provides a simplified version of BS 7671 for common electrical installations.
terms.misconception: Thinking it replaces the full BS 7671 for complex or industrial jobs.

teachChecks.title: The Legal Hierarchy
teachChecks.objective: Define the fundamental difference between statutory and non-statutory requirements.
retrievalTextLines: In the UK electrical industry, documents are split into two main groups based on their legal power. Statutory regulations are the 'law of the land.' These are Acts of Parliament that you must follow by law; they are mandatory. If you break these, you are committing a criminal offense which can lead to legal action in a court of law.

On the other hand, non-statutory regulations are 'codes of practice' or technical guidance. These are not laws themselves, but they provide the specific instructions on how to work safely to meet the requirements of the law. Think of statutory regulations as the 'what you must do' (for example, keep people safe) and non-statutory as the 'how to do it' (for example, what size cable to use for a specific circuit). While statutory is the legal framework, non-statutory is the industry's technical manual.
teachChecks.hint: Think about which one is an Act of Parliament and which one is a guide on how to work.

teachChecks.title: Legal Status vs. Technical Guidance
teachChecks.objective: Explain how non-statutory guidance supports statutory requirements.
retrievalTextLines: Even though non-statutory documents like BS 7671 (the IET Wiring Regulations) are not Acts of Parliament, they are not 'optional' in a practical sense. They are used in court to show whether an electrician has met the legal standards set by statutory laws. For example, the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is a statutory law that says electrical systems must be constructed to prevent danger. However, the EAWR does not tell you exactly how many sockets to put on a circuit or how deep to bury a cable.

To meet that legal requirement, you follow the non-statutory guidance in BS 7671. If an accident occurs and a case goes to court, a judge will look at whether you followed the non-statutory guidance to decide if you obeyed the statutory law. Following the guidance is the primary way to prove you are working to a safe, legal standard.
teachChecks.hint: Consider how a judge uses a technical guide to decide if a law was broken.

teachChecks.title: Identifying Statutory Regulations
teachChecks.objective: Identify specific statutory regulations relevant to the electrical industry.
retrievalTextLines: You must recognize specific statutory regulations by their names. The most important is the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), which is the 'umbrella' law for all workplace safety. Underneath this, we have the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), which is the primary law for our trade.

Other statutory laws you must identify include: PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) for using tools safely; COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) for handling chemicals or dust; and the PPE (Personal Protective Equipment at Work) Regulations. You also need to know the ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations), which concerns the supply of electricity, and the CDM (Construction Design and Management) Regulations. Other examples include the Environmental Act, the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act), and the Manual Handling Operations Regulations. All of these are mandatory legal requirements.
teachChecks.hint: Look for names that include 'Act' or 'Regulations' and cover broad safety or legal rights.

teachChecks.title: Identifying Non-Statutory Guidance
teachChecks.objective: Identify specific non-statutory regulations and guidance notes.
retrievalTextLines: Non-statutory documents are the technical tools you use every day to design and install systems. The most famous is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' Because it is a British Standard (BS), it is non-statutory, not a law. Another vital document is the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified version of BS 7671 for common domestic and small commercial jobs.

You will also use Guidance Notes (GN), such as Guidance Note 3, which focuses specifically on inspection and testing. Other non-statutory sources include the Unite Union Book (often used for site rates and conditions) and various industry Codes of Practice. These documents provide the 'how-to' details that the statutory laws leave out. If a document is published by the IET (Institution of Engineering and Technology) or the BSI (British Standards Institution), it is almost always non-statutory guidance.
teachChecks.hint: Look for British Standards (BS), Guidance Notes, or the On-Site Guide.

teachChecks.title: Categorization Mastery
teachChecks.objective: Correctly sort a mixed list of industry documents into statutory and non-statutory categories.
retrievalTextLines: To tell the difference quickly, look at the titles and the purpose of the document. Statutory regulations usually end in 'Act' or 'Regulations' and are issued by the government to set a legal minimum. Examples include the Noise at Work Regulations and the Equal Opportunities legislation. These focus on the legal duties of employers and employees.

Non-statutory documents are often technical manuals. While the EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) is a statutory law, the 'Wiring Regulations' (BS 7671) is a non-statutory standard. This is a common point of confusion: just because BS 7671 has the word 'Regulations' in its title, it does not make it a statutory Act of Parliament. Similarly, any document labeled as a 'Guidance Note' or 'Code of Practice' is non-statutory. Keeping these categories clear ensures you know which document provides the mandatory legal framework and which provides the technical instruction needed to comply with that framework.
teachChecks.hint: Remember that BS 7671 is a standard (non-statutory), while the EAWR is a law (statutory).

teachChecks.title: The Legal Hierarchy

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the term for mandatory Acts of Parliament that are known as the 'law of the land'?
What is the term for industry documents that act as technical 'how-to' guides or codes of practice?
What type of offense is committed if an electrician breaks a statutory regulation?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why is it necessary to have both statutory and non-statutory documents in the electrical industry rather than just one type?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Statutory regulations set the broad legal requirement or framework
Non-statutory regulations provide the specific technical instructions
Statutory tells you what to do while non-statutory tells you how to do it
One provides the law and the other provides the technical manual to meet that law
teachChecks.hint: Think about the difference between a legal requirement and a technical instruction.

teachChecks.title: Legal Status vs. Technical Guidance

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory law mandates that electrical systems must be constructed to prevent danger?
What is the non-statutory national standard used for the design and installation of electrical wiring?
Where is non-statutory guidance used to prove that an electrician has met legal standards?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: How does following the non-statutory BS 7671 help an electrician comply with the statutory Electricity at Work Regulations?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It provides the technical details that the EAWR law leaves out
It acts as evidence in court that safe standards were followed
Following the guidance is the primary way to prove the legal standard was met
It translates the legal duty to prevent danger into specific installation rules
teachChecks.hint: Consider how a judge uses a technical standard to evaluate if a law was followed.

teachChecks.title: Identifying Statutory Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation acts as the 'umbrella' law for all workplace safety in the UK?
answerGuidance: Health and Safety at Work Act

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation covers the safe use of tools and work equipment?
answerGuidance: Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations

basicQuestions.questionText: Which statutory regulation is concerned with the handling of chemicals or dust?
answerGuidance: Control of Substances Hazardous to Health

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: Why are the PPE Regulations and Manual Handling Operations Regulations classified as statutory rather than non-statutory?
deeperAnswerGuidance: They are mandatory legal requirements passed by Parliament
They set a legal minimum for safety that must be followed by law
They are not just technical guides but are enforceable Acts or Regulations
Failure to follow them can lead to criminal prosecution
teachChecks.hint: Think about their legal origin and whether they are optional guidance or mandatory law.

teachChecks.title: Identifying Non-Statutory Guidance

basicQuestions.questionText: What is the common name for the simplified version of BS 7671 used for common domestic installations?
Which non-statutory document focuses specifically on the inspection and testing of installations?
Which organization, alongside the BSI, is a primary publisher of non-statutory electrical guidance?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: If BS 7671 is non-statutory, why is it often referred to as the 'electrician's bible' in a practical sense?
deeperAnswerGuidance: It is the primary technical tool used for daily design and installation
It provides the essential 'how-to' details for the trade
It is the national standard that everyone in the industry must follow to be safe
It contains the specific technical rules that ensure compliance with the law
teachChecks.hint: Focus on its role as a technical manual for daily work.

teachChecks.title: Categorization Mastery

basicQuestions.questionText: To which category does the Noise at Work Regulations belong?
To which category does a document labeled as a 'Code of Practice' belong?
What is the legal category of BS 7671, even though it has 'Regulations' in its title?

teachChecks.deeperQuestionText: What is the most reliable way to distinguish a statutory regulation from a non-statutory guidance note based on its title and origin?
deeperAnswerGuidance: Statutory regulations are usually Acts of Parliament or government-issued laws
Non-statutory documents are often British Standards (BS) or Guidance Notes
Statutory titles often end in 'Act' or 'Regulations' and set legal duties
Non-statutory documents are technical manuals published by bodies like the IET or BSI
teachChecks.hint: Look at who publishes the document and whether it is an Act of Parliament.

parsed.title: Worked Example
parsed.objective: Demonstrate how to classify the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and BS 7671 by looking at their legal origin and purpose.
retrievalTextLines: Scenario: You are preparing for a site inspection and need to explain to an apprentice why we use both the 'Electricity at Work Regulations' (EAWR) and 'BS 7671' (The Wiring Regulations).

Step 1: Identify the legal origin. Look at who issued the document. The EAWR is a set of regulations passed by Parliament, making it an Act of the land. BS 7671 is published by the Institution of Engineering and Technology (IET) and the British Standards Institution (BSI).

Step 2: Determine the purpose. Ask yourself: 'Does this set a broad legal duty or a specific technical instruction?' The EAWR states that systems must be safe to prevent danger (a broad legal duty). BS 7671 gives the specific numbers, such as disconnection times and cable capacities (technical instructions).

Step 3: Categorize the documents. Because the EAWR is a mandatory law from Parliament, it is Statutory. Because BS 7671 is a technical standard published by industry bodies to provide guidance, it is Non-statutory.
Final Result: The Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is a Statutory Regulation. BS 7671 (The Wiring Regulations) is a Non-statutory Regulation.

Takeaway: Don't let the word 'Regulations' in the title of BS 7671 fool you. If it's a British Standard (BS) or a guide on how to install, it's non-statutory. If it's an Act of Parliament that sets a legal requirement, it's statutory.
parsed.hint: Focus on who wrote the document and whether it sets a legal duty or a technical rule.

practice.hint: Think about which one is a law regarding hazardous substances and which one is a specific guide for inspection and testing.

steps.title: The Legal Hierarchy
steps.objective: Define the fundamental difference between statutory and non-statutory requirements.
keyIdeas: Statutory regulations are laws passed by Parliament; non-statutory regulations are codes of practice or guidance.
Electricians must know which documents are legally mandatory versus those that provide technical guidance to meet the law.

In the UK electrical industry, documents are split into two main groups based on their legal power. Statutory regulations are the 'law of the land.' These are Acts of Parliament that you must follow by law; they are mandatory. If you break these, you are committing a criminal offense which can lead to legal action in a court of law.

On the other hand, non-statutory regulations are 'codes of practice' or technical guidance. These are not laws themselves, but they provide the specific instructions on how to work safely to meet the requirements of the law. Think of statutory regulations as the 'what you must do' (for example, keep people safe) and non-statutory as the 'how to do it' (for example, what size cable to use for a specific circuit). While statutory is the legal framework, non-statutory is the industry's technical manual.
Explanation A
anchorFacts: In the UK electrical industry, documents are split into two main groups based on their legal power. Statutory regulations are the 'law of the land.' These are Acts of Parliament that you must follow by law; they are mandatory. If you break these, you are committing a criminal offense which can lead to legal action in a court of law.

On the other hand, non-statutory regulations are 'codes of practice' or technical guidance. These are not laws themselves, but they provide the specific instructions on how to work safely to meet the requirements of the law. Think of statutory regulations as the 'what you must do' (for example, keep people safe) and non-statutory as the 'how to do it' (for example, what size cable to use for a specific circuit). While statutory is the legal framework, non-statutory is the industry's technical manual.
misconceptionsToWatch: Wrong belief: All industry documents have the same legal status.
Think about which one is an Act of Parliament and which one is a guide on how to work.

steps.title: Legal Status vs. Technical Guidance
steps.objective: Explain how non-statutory guidance supports statutory requirements.
keyIdeas: Non-statutory documents like BS 7671 are used to demonstrate compliance with statutory laws like the EAWR.
It prevents the mistake of thinking guidance is 'optional' just because it isn't a direct Act of Parliament.

Even though non-statutory documents like BS 7671 (the IET Wiring Regulations) are not Acts of Parliament, they are not 'optional' in a practical sense. They are used in court to show whether an electrician has met the legal standards set by statutory laws. For example, the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) is a statutory law that says electrical systems must be constructed to prevent danger. However, the EAWR does not tell you exactly how many sockets to put on a circuit or how deep to bury a cable.

To meet that legal requirement, you follow the non-statutory guidance in BS 7671. If an accident occurs and a case goes to court, a judge will look at whether you followed the non-statutory guidance to decide if you obeyed the statutory law. Following the guidance is the primary way to prove you are working to a safe, legal standard.

steps.title: Identifying Statutory Regulations
steps.objective: Identify specific statutory regulations relevant to the electrical industry.
keyTerms: Health and Safety at Work Act
keyIdeas: HASAWA, EAWR, ESQCR, PUWER, COSHH, CDM, Manual Handling, PPE, Noise at Work, Environmental Act, DDA, and Equal Opportunities.
Failure to comply with these specific Acts can lead to criminal prosecution.

You must recognize specific statutory regulations by their names. The most important is the Health and Safety at Work Act (HASAWA), which is the 'umbrella' law for all workplace safety. Underneath this, we have the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR), which is the primary law for our trade.

Other statutory laws you must identify include: PUWER (Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations) for using tools safely; COSHH (Control of Substances Hazardous to Health) for handling chemicals or dust; and the PPE (Personal Protective Equipment at Work) Regulations. You also need to know the ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations), which concerns the supply of electricity, and the CDM (Construction Design and Management) Regulations. Other examples include the Environmental Act, the DDA (Disability Discrimination Act), and the Manual Handling Operations Regulations. All of these are mandatory legal requirements.

steps.title: Identifying Non-Statutory Guidance
steps.objective: Identify specific non-statutory regulations and guidance notes.
keyIdeas: BS 7671 (IET Wiring Regulations), On-Site Guide, Unite Union Book, Guidance Notes, and Codes of Practice.
These are the primary technical tools used daily for design, installation, and testing.

Non-statutory documents are the technical tools you use every day to design and install systems. The most famous is BS 7671, also known as the IET Wiring Regulations or 'The Big Blue Book.' Because it is a British Standard (BS), it is non-statutory, not a law. Another vital document is the On-Site Guide, which is a simplified version of BS 7671 for common domestic and small commercial jobs.

You will also use Guidance Notes (GN), such as Guidance Note 3, which focuses specifically on inspection and testing. Other non-statutory sources include the Unite Union Book (often used for site rates and conditions) and various industry Codes of Practice. These documents provide the 'how-to' details that the statutory laws leave out. If a document is published by the IET (Institution of Engineering and Technology) or the BSI (British Standards Institution), it is almost always non-statutory guidance.

steps.title: Categorization Mastery
steps.objective: Correctly sort a mixed list of industry documents into statutory and non-statutory categories.
keyIdeas: Synthesizing the full range of AC1.1 and AC1.2 into a single classification framework.
Ensures the learner can accurately identify the nature of any document they encounter on site.

To tell the difference quickly, look at the titles and the purpose of the document. Statutory regulations usually end in 'Act' or 'Regulations' and are issued by the government to set a legal minimum. Examples include the Noise at Work Regulations and the Equal Opportunities legislation. These focus on the legal duties of employers and employees.

Non-statutory documents are often technical manuals. While the EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) is a statutory law, the 'Wiring Regulations' (BS 7671) is a non-statutory standard. This is a common point of confusion: just because BS 7671 has the word 'Regulations' in its title, it does not make it a statutory Act of Parliament. Similarly, any document labeled as a 'Guidance Note' or 'Code of Practice' is non-statutory. Keeping these categories clear ensures you know which document provides the mandatory legal framework and which provides the technical instruction needed to comply with that framework.

steps.title: Worked Example
steps.objective: Demonstrate how to classify the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and BS 7671 by looking at their legal origin and purpose.
keyIdeas: Demonstrate how to classify the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) and BS 7671 by looking at their legal origin and purpose.
Show one worked example.
Scenario: You are preparing for a site inspection and need to explain to an apprentice why we use both the 'Electricity at Work Regulations' (EAWR) and 'BS 7671' (The Wiring Regulations).

Step 1: Identify the legal origin. Look at who issued the document. The EAWR is a set of regulations passed by Parliament, making it an Act of the land. BS 7671 is published by the Institution of Engineering and Technology (IET) and the British Standards Institution (BSI).

Step 2: Determine the purpose. Ask yourself: 'Does this set a broad legal duty or a specific technical instruction?' The EAWR states that systems must be safe to prevent danger (a broad legal duty). BS 7671 gives the specific numbers, such as disconnection times and cable capacities (technical instructions).

Step 3: Categorize the documents. Because the EAWR is a mandatory law from Parliament, it is Statutory. Because BS 7671 is a technical standard published by industry bodies to provide guidance, it is Non-statutory.
